National Institute of Teaching  ·  Research & Evidence
Evidence Briefing  ·  April 2026

Who Gets a Good Pass? Mapping Disadvantaged Pupils' GCSE Outcomes in English Secondary Schools

Fewer than three in ten disadvantaged pupils achieve a grade 5+ in English and Maths. The picture is far worse for White British pupils on free school meals — and the schools beating the odds are telling us why.

Calum Davey  ·  April 2026

Full briefing with technical notes available as a PDF

↓ Download PDF

The Gap That Isn't Closing

Every year, fewer than three in ten disadvantaged pupils in England achieve a "good pass" — grade 5 or above in both GCSE English Language and Mathematics. Among their better-off classmates, it is more than five in ten.

That gap — around 27 percentage points — is wider today than it was before the pandemic. Despite billions of pounds in Pupil Premium funding, despite decades of school improvement effort, and despite a stated government commitment to halve it, the gap has barely moved.

But the aggregate figure conceals something important. The disadvantage gap is not the same for all pupils. Ethnicity is a powerful additional factor — and White British pupils on free school meals are near the bottom of every measure.

The National Picture

Good pass: GCSE grade 5+ in both English Language and Mathematics — the government's "strong pass" threshold. Disadvantaged: Pupils eligible for free school meals at any point in the past six years, or looked after (the Pupil Premium definition).

26% Disadvantaged pupils
achieving a good pass
53% Non-disadvantaged pupils
achieving a good pass
27pp The gap between
these two groups
18% White British FSM pupils
achieving a good pass

The Schools White Paper (February 2026) commits to halving this gap — from ~27pp to ~13–14pp. The gap has widened since 2019, not narrowed.

What 3,000 Schools Tell Us

The chart below shows every non-selective secondary school in England with 2024–25 data — around 3,100 schools. The vertical dashed line marks 60% — the threshold used here to identify genuinely outstanding performance for disadvantaged pupils.

Most schools cluster around the 26% national average. The 52 schools to the right of the red line — achieving ≥60% for disadvantaged pupils — are the focus of this briefing.

Where Are the High-Performing Schools?

London dominates — two-thirds of the 52 schools are in the capital. Most belong to strong academy chains: Star Academies (8 schools), Ark, Harris Federation. Half are faith schools. Outside London, equivalent schools are almost entirely absent from the Midlands, South West, and East of England.

The Ethnicity Dimension: White British Pupils

White British pupils on free school meals achieve just 18% — the second lowest of any named ethnic group, far below the 26% average for all disadvantaged pupils, and far below FSM-eligible Bangladeshi (49%) or Black African (42%) pupils.

Ethnic groupFSM pass rateNon-FSM pass rateFSM gap
Chinese75%79%3pp
Bangladeshi49%61%12pp
Indian49%72%24pp
Black African42%54%12pp
Pakistani36%50%13pp
Caribbean22%37%15pp
White British18%49%31pp
Gypsy/Roma3%13%9pp

The FSM/non-FSM gap for White British pupils is 31pp — among the largest of any group. Being on free school meals is a particularly powerful predictor of low attainment for White British pupils specifically.

At school level, the same pattern holds. Schools with higher proportions of White British pupils tend to have lower pass rates for their disadvantaged pupils (r = −0.28 across 3,182 schools).

Bringing It Together: Who Are the 52 Schools Serving?

In the typical English secondary school, 62% of pupils are White British. In the 52 high-performing schools, that figure is just 26%.

Ethnic groupHigh-performing schoolsAll other schoolsDifference
White British26%62%−36pp
Asian33%12%+21pp
Black13%7%+6pp
Mixed10%7%+3pp
Other White9%7%+2pp

This finding demands careful interpretation. It does not mean that schools succeed simply by having fewer White British pupils. It means three things:

First, high-performing schools are disproportionately serving communities where disadvantaged pupils from minority ethnic groups — particularly South Asian groups — tend to achieve relatively well. Bangladeshi, Indian, and Black African FSM pupils all substantially outperform White British FSM pupils nationally. Schools with high proportions of these pupils will, by definition, show higher average pass rates for "disadvantaged pupils" as a group.

Second, the high-performing schools are still genuinely impressive. Even within minority ethnic groups, FSM pass rates are typically well below 50%. Schools clearing 60% for their whole disadvantaged cohort are doing something real — the chains that run them apply structured, evidence-based approaches across ethnic groups.

Third — and most importantly — there are very few high-performing schools for disadvantaged White British pupils. Those schools are largely absent from the post-industrial towns and coastal communities where White British FSM pupils are most concentrated. The absence of high-performing schools in those areas is a failure of the system to develop effective approaches for this specific population.

Implications for the National Institute of Teaching

  1. The gap is structural, not just instructional. High-performing schools combine strong curriculum design, coherent CPD, effective Pupil Premium use, and sustained leadership commitment. NIoT's teacher development offer must be embedded in this broader ecology.
  2. The regional distribution is a challenge. Two-thirds of high-performing schools are in London. The areas with the worst outcomes — post-industrial towns, coastal communities, the East Midlands — have the highest concentrations of White British disadvantaged pupils and the fewest effective schools. NIoT's national footprint should prioritise these regions.
  3. Ethnicity must be part of the framing. Treating "disadvantaged pupils" as a single group conceals the specific crisis of White British FSM pupils. Approaches proven in diverse urban schools may not transfer to predominantly White British communities.
  4. The chain effect is real — but geographically limited. Star Academies, Ark, and Harris have demonstrated improvement at scale. Understanding how their approaches do or don't translate to predominantly White British school communities is a live question for NIoT.
  5. The hardest work is in the whitest towns. Halving the disadvantage gap nationally requires building a new evidence base around what works for White British disadvantaged pupils — in Barnsley, Stoke, Grimsby, and the dozens of similar communities where the current system is most conspicuously failing them. This is where NIoT's most distinctive contribution could lie.

Technical Notes

School-level attainment: DfE KS4 Performance tables, 2024–25 (provisional). "Good pass" = grade 5+ in both English Language and Mathematics. "Disadvantaged" = ever-FSM in past 6 years + looked-after children. Non-selective schools via GIAS. n = 3,110 schools with non-suppressed data.

National figures (26%/53%/27pp): DfE KS4 2023–24 — most recent published national breakdown by disadvantage group.

Ethnicity × FSM: Social Mobility Commission / DfE Attainment at Age 16 dataset, 2022–23. School ethnic composition from DfE Schools, Pupils and Their Characteristics 2024–25 (January 2025 census). Joined by URN; 3,232 schools matched for scatter; 52 vs 3,036 for composition comparison.

Map: England LA boundaries from martinjc/UK-GeoJSON. Coordinates from GIAS (OSGB36 → WGS84). Analysis: Python (pandas, matplotlib, pyproj). April 2026.

Prepared April 2026  ·  Data: Department for Education, GIAS, Social Mobility Commission
